topics.title: Day 1: The OSI Model
topics.description: Understand the 7-layer OSI model and its security implications
topics.content:
## Day 1: The OSI Model

> **Why This Gets You Hired:** Every single network attack operates at one or more OSI layers. SOC analysts use this model daily to identify where an attack is happening, which logs to check, and which defence to deploy. Interviewers ask OSI layer questions to test whether you understand how networks actually work under the hood.

:::objectives
- Name all seven OSI layers in order and explain what each one does
- Identify which layer a given attack or protocol operates at
- Understand how data encapsulation works as it moves down and up the stack
- Apply OSI layer knowledge to choose the right security control
:::

## The Big Picture

The Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) model is a **conceptual framework** that divides network communication into seven distinct layers. Think of it as a **stack** � data starts at the top (your application), travels down through each layer, gets sent across the wire, then travels back up on the receiving end.

```
Application  (Layer 7)  ? You are here
Presentation (Layer 6)
Session      (Layer 5)
Transport    (Layer 4)  ? TCP/UDP live here
Network      (Layer 3)  ? IP addresses live here
Data Link    (Layer 2)  ? MAC addresses live here
Physical     (Layer 1)  ? Actual wires/radio waves
```

:::info **Why layers matter for security:**
Each layer has its own attack vectors and defences. A SYN flood targets Layer 4. ARP spoofing targets Layer 2. SQL injection targets Layer 7. When you know which layer an attack hits, you know exactly which tool to use and which log to check.
:::

## Layer 1 � Physical (The Wire)

**What it does:** Transmits raw bits (0s and 1s) over physical media � copper wires, fibre optics, or radio waves.

**Hardware:** Hubs, repeaters, cables (cat5e, cat6, fibre), antennas, NICs.

**Security threats:**
- Cable tapping (physical access to wire = read all traffic)
- Rogue devices plugged into open ports
- Electromagnetic interference / signal jamming

**Defences:** Locked server rooms, port security on switches (only allow specific MACs), tamper-detecting cables.

```bash
# On a Cisco switch, port security limits how many MACs per port:
switchport port-security
switchport port-security maximum 1
switchport port-security violation shutdown
```

:::tip **Remember it:** Layer 1 = Physical = "The Pipe" � if you can touch it, it's Layer 1.
:::

## Layer 2 � Data Link (The Neighbourhood)

**What it does:** Organises raw bits into **frames** with MAC addresses. Handles communication between directly connected devices on the same network segment.

**Hardware:** Switches, bridges. Data unit = **Frame** (header: src MAC, dst MAC, payload).

**Key protocol:** **ARP** (Address Resolution Protocol) � translates IP addresses to MAC addresses.

**Security threats:**
- **ARP spoofing:** Attacker sends fake ARP replies, mapping their MAC to the gateway's IP. Now all traffic goes through them (man-in-the-middle).
- **MAC flooding:** Flood switch with fake MACs until it fails open (hub mode), allowing traffic sniffing.
- **STP manipulation:** Spanning Tree Protocol attacks to reroute traffic.

**Defences:**
- DHCP snooping (validates which ports can send DHCP messages)
- Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) � validates ARP packets
- Port security on switches

```bash
# Show ARP table on Linux/Mac:
arp -a

# Show ARP table on Windows:
arp -a
```

:::warning **Real case � ARP spoofing in the wild:**
In 2019, a bank's branch network was compromised when an attacker plugged a Raspberry Pi into an open ethernet port. The Pi ran ARP spoofing to redirect branch traffic through itself, capturing credentials and then pivoting to the core banking network. **Unlocked network ports + no DHCP snooping = breach.**
:::

## Layer 3 � Network (The Postal Service)

**What it does:** Routes packets between different networks using **IP addresses**. This is where the internet lives.

**Hardware:** Routers, Layer 3 switches. Data unit = **Packet**.

**Key protocols:** IP (IPv4, IPv6), ICMP (ping), ARP (also here for routing).

**Security threats:**
- **IP spoofing:** Forging the source IP address in a packet to bypass access controls
- **Routing attacks:** BGP hijacking, route poisoning
- **ICMP attacks:** Ping floods, Smurf attacks (amplification via broadcast)
- **Fragmentation attacks:** Overlapping fragments to bypass IDS

**Defences:**
- Ingress/egress filtering (RFC 2827) � drop packets with spoofed source IPs
- ACLs on routers � permit/deny traffic by subnet
- IPsec for authenticated communication
- Rate limiting on ICMP

```bash
# Trace the route packets take to google.com:
traceroute google.com
# (Each hop is a Layer 3 device)
```

## Layer 4 � Transport (The Delivery Guarantee)

**What it does:** Manages end-to-end communication between applications. Chooses between **reliable delivery** (TCP) and **fast delivery** (UDP).

**Key protocols:**
- **TCP** � Connection-oriented. Three-way handshake (SYN ? SYN-ACK ? ACK). Guarantees delivery via sequence numbers and acknowledgements. Used by HTTP, SSH, SMTP.
- **UDP** � Connectionless. Fire-and-forget. Used by DNS, DHCP, VoIP, video streaming.

**Security threats:**
- **SYN flood:** Attacker sends many SYN packets without completing handshake, exhausting server resources.
- **TCP reset attack:** Forged RST packet tears down a legitimate connection.
- **UDP amplification:** Small DNS query to open resolver triggers large response to victim (DDoS vector).
- **Port scanning:** Attacker probes which TCP/UDP ports are open.

**Defences:**
- SYN cookies (mitigate SYN floods)
- Stateful firewalls (track connection state)
- Rate limiting on UDP services

:::concept TCP Three-Way Handshake
1. **SYN:** Client sends SYN packet with initial sequence number (ISN).
2. **SYN-ACK:** Server responds with SYN + its own ISN, acknowledges client ISN.
3. **ACK:** Client acknowledges server ISN. Connection established.

Security significance: A half-open scan (SYN scan) sends SYN and never ACKs. This is stealthier than a full connect scan because the connection is never fully established.
:::

## Layer 5 � Session (The Conversation Manager)

**What it does:** Manages sessions between applications � establishes, maintains, and tears down connections.

**Examples:** NetBIOS, RPC, PPTP, SIP (VoIP).

**Security threats:**
- **Session hijacking:** Stealing a valid session token to impersonate a user.
- **Session fixation:** Forcing a user to use a session ID controlled by the attacker.
- **Man-in-the-middle (MITM):** Intercepting and modifying session data.

**Defences:**
- Session timeouts and rotation of session IDs
- TLS encryption protects session data at lower layers
- Secure cookies with HttpOnly and SameSite flags

## Layer 6 � Presentation (The Translator)

**What it does:** Translates data between application format and network format. Handles **encryption**, **compression**, and **character encoding**.

**Examples:** SSL/TLS (encryption at this layer), JPEG, GIF, ASCII/Unicode, EBCDIC.

**Security threats:**
- **SSL stripping:** Downgrading HTTPS to HTTP (man-in-the-middle drops TLS)
- **Encoding exploits:** Character encoding mismatches that bypass input validation

**Defences:**
- Always enforce HTTPS with HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security)
- Validate input after decoding, not before

:::tip **Common mistake:**
Developers sometimes validate input before URL-decoding it. An attacker can URL-encode malicious payload to bypass filters. **Always decode first, then validate.**
:::

## Layer 7 � Application (The User Facing Part)

**What it does:** The layer applications use to communicate over the network. This is what users interact with.

**Examples:** HTTP/HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, DNS, SSH, DHCP.

**Security threats (this is the most attacked layer):**
- **SQL injection:** Malicious SQL in web form inputs
- **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS):** Injecting scripts into web pages
- **Buffer overflows:** Exploiting application memory handling
- **DNS tunnelling:** Encapsulating data in DNS queries to bypass firewalls
- **Phishing:** Social engineering via email (SMTP)

**Defences:**
- Web Application Firewall (WAF)
- Input validation and parameterised queries
- Content Security Policy (CSP) headers
- DNSSEC and DNS filtering

## The Mnemonic � Never Forget the Layers

From bottom to top (Layer 1 ? 7):
```
Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away
�     �  �   �    �       �       �
P-D-N-T-S-P-A   ? First letters of each layer
```

From top to bottom (Layer 7 ? 1):
```
All People Seem To Need Data Processing
�    �     �    �  �   �    �     �
A-P-S-T-N-D-P   ? Reverse order
```

## Putting It All Together � How Data Flows

When you visit `https://google.com`:

1. **Layer 7 (Application):** Browser sends HTTP GET request
2. **Layer 6 (Presentation):** TLS encrypts the HTTP data
3. **Layer 5 (Session):** Manages the TLS session
4. **Layer 4 (Transport):** TCP segments the data, adds port 443 (destination), assigns source port
5. **Layer 3 (Network):** IP wraps segments in packets with source/destination IP addresses. ARP resolves next-hop MAC
6. **Layer 2 (Data Link):** Frames packets with MAC addresses, CRC checksum
7. **Layer 1 (Physical):** Transmits bits as electrical signals over ethernet cable

The receiving end does the reverse: bits ? frame ? packet ? segment ? data.

:::warning **Security takeaway:**
- HTTP attack (Layer 7) ? Check web server logs, use WAF
- SYN flood (Layer 4) ? Check netstat/ss, use SYN cookies
- ARP spoof (Layer 2) ? Check switch logs, enable DAI
- Cable tap (Layer 1) ? Lock the server room

**The layer tells you where to look and what to use.**
:::

## Hands-On: Explore the Layers

Open the Virtual Terminal and run these commands to see the layers in action:

```bash
# Layer 3 � See your IP address and routing table:
ip a
ip route

# Layer 4 � See active TCP connections:
ss -tlnp

# Layer 3/4 � Trace the path to google.com:
traceroute google.com

# Layer 2 � View ARP table:
arp -a
```

:::summary
- **Layer 1 (Physical):** Wires, radio, bits. Defend with physical security
- **Layer 2 (Data Link):** MAC addresses, frames, switches. Defend with port security and DAI
- **Layer 3 (Network):** IP addresses, packets, routers. Defend with ACLs and filtering
- **Layer 4 (Transport):** TCP/UDP, ports, segments. Defend with firewalls and SYN cookies
- **Layer 5 (Session):** Sessions, checkpoints. Defend with session management
- **Layer 6 (Presentation):** Encryption, encoding. Defend with TLS
- **Layer 7 (Application):** HTTP, DNS, apps. Defend with WAF and input validation
- **Key skill:** When you see an attack, identify its layer ? pick the right defence
:::
topics.aiPrompt: Explain how a SQL injection attack traverses the OSI model layers from application to network and back
topics.interviewAnswer: Layer 6 (Presentation) handles encryption, compression, and translation between application and network formats.

quiz.question: How many layers in the OSI model?
quiz.explanation: The OSI model has 7 layers: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application.

quiz.question: Which layer handles IP addressing and routing?
quiz.explanation: Layer 3 (Network) handles IP addressing, routing, and packet forwarding. Routers operate at this layer.

quiz.question: At which layer does TCP operate?
quiz.explanation: TCP operates at Layer 4 (Transport). It provides reliable, connection-oriented data delivery between applications.

quiz.question: What is a SYN flood attack targetting?
quiz.explanation: SYN floods target Layer 4 (Transport) by sending many TCP SYN packets without completing the handshake, exhausting server connection tables.

quiz.question: What security control would you use against ARP spoofing?
quiz.explanation: Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) validates ARP packets against the DHCP snooping database, preventing ARP spoofing attacks at Layer 2.

quiz.question: At what layer does a Web Application Firewall (WAF) operate?
quiz.explanation: WAFs operate at Layer 7 (Application) to inspect HTTP/HTTPS traffic for web application attacks like SQL injection and XSS.

quiz.question: What protocol resolves IP addresses to MAC addresses?
quiz.explanation: ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) at Layer 2 maps IP addresses to MAC addresses on the local network.

quiz.question: Which OSI layer is responsible for encryption?
quiz.explanation: Layer 6 (Presentation) handles encryption, compression, and data format translation. TLS/SSL operates at this layer.

quiz.question: What data unit is used at Layer 2?
quiz.explanation: Layer 2 (Data Link) organises data into frames with MAC addresses and CRC checksums. Packets are Layer 3, segments are Layer 4.

quiz.question: Which attack is an example of a Layer 2 attack?
quiz.explanation: ARP spoofing is a Layer 2 (Data Link) attack where the attacker sends forged ARP replies to intercept traffic.

quiz.question: How does HTTPS protect data at which layers?
quiz.explanation: HTTPS (HTTP over TLS) operates at Layer 7, while TLS provides encryption at Layer 6 (Presentation) and session management at Layer 5 (Session).

quiz.question: What is the purpose of the TCP three-way handshake?
quiz.explanation: The three-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) establishes a reliable TCP connection by synchronising sequence numbers between sender and receiver.

quiz.question: Which device operates at Layer 3?
quiz.explanation: Routers operate at Layer 3 (Network) making forwarding decisions based on IP addresses. Switches are Layer 2, hubs/repeaters are Layer 1.

quiz.question: What is encapsulation in the OSI model?
options: Each layer adding its own header
quiz.explanation: Encapsulation means each layer wraps the data from the layer above with its own header (and sometimes trailer), like Russian nesting dolls.

quiz.question: You see a port scan in your logs � which layer is being probed?
quiz.explanation: Port scans probe Layer 4 (Transport) ports to discover which services are listening. A SYN scan sends SYN packets to different ports and watches for SYN-ACK responses.

topics.title: Day 2: The TCP/IP Model
topics.description: Learn the TCP/IP protocol suite and its relation to network security
topics.content:
## Day 2: The TCP/IP Model

> **Why This Gets You Hired:** The TCP/IP model is what actually runs the internet. Every firewall rule you write, every packet you analyse, and every network attack you investigate involves TCP/IP. SOC analysts think in TCP/IP layers, penetration testers craft packets at specific layers, and every networking interview question traces back to this model. Mastering TCP/IP is non-negotiable.

:::objectives
- Identify the four layers of the TCP/IP model and how they map to the OSI model
- Analyse the TCP three-way handshake and explain its security significance
- Distinguish between TCP and UDP and when each is used
- Recognise common attacks targeting each TCP/IP layer
:::

## The Four-Layer Model

The TCP/IP model condenses the OSI model’s seven layers into four practical layers. This is the model that actually powers the internet, not a theoretical framework.

```
OSI Model            TCP/IP Model         Protocols
---------            ------------         ---------
Application  ─┐
Presentation ─┤  Application Layer      HTTP, DNS, SMTP, SSH
Session      ─┘
Transport       Transport Layer         TCP, UDP
Network         Internet Layer          IP, ICMP, ARP
Data Link   ─┐
Physical    ─┘  Link Layer             Ethernet, Wi-Fi
```

## Layer 1 — Link Layer (OSI 1-2)

**What it does:** Handles physical transmission and local network addressing.

**Key Protocols:** Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (802.11), ARP.

**Security:** ARP spoofing, MAC flooding, STP manipulation.

:::warning **Layer 2 Attacks**
- ARP spoofing: forged ARP replies redirect traffic through attacker
- MAC flooding: exhausts switch CAM tables, forcing hub mode
- Defence: DAI, DHCP snooping, port security
:::

## Layer 2 — Internet Layer (OSI 3)

**Key Protocols:** IPv4, IPv6, ICMP.

Security: IP spoofing, ICMP tunnelling, fragmentation attacks.

## Layer 3 — Transport Layer (OSI 4)

**TCP:** Connection-oriented, reliable. Three-way handshake: SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK.

**UDP:** Connectionless, no ordering. Used by DNS, DHCP.

**Security:** SYN floods, TCP reset, UDP amplification.

## Layer 4 — Application Layer (OSI 5-7)

**Key Protocols:** HTTP, HTTPS, DNS, SMTP, SSH.

**Security:** Most attacks happen here (SQLi, XSS, command injection).

:::summary
- TCP/IP has four layers: Link, Internet, Transport, Application
- TCP is reliable with a three-way handshake
- UDP is fast but easy to spoof
- Understanding layers tells you which defence to use
:::

:::checkpoint
How many layers does the TCP/IP model have?
A) 7
B) 5
C) 4
D) 3
Correct: C
:::

:::checkpoint
A SYN scan sends SYN and then what?
A) Sends ACK
B) Sends RST after SYN-ACK
C) Sends FIN
D) Waits for timeout
Correct: B
:::
topics.aiPrompt: Compare and contrast the OSI and TCP/IP models, explaining why TCP/IP is the practical model used on the internet
topics.interviewQuestion: What is the purpose of the TCP three-way handshake?
topics.interviewAnswer: It establishes a reliable connection between two hosts by synchronising sequence numbers (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) before data transfer begins.

topics.title: Day 3: Subnetting and Network Segmentation
topics.description: Master subnet calculations and network segmentation for security isolation
topics.content:
## Day 3: Subnetting and Network Segmentation

> **Why This Gets You Hired:** Subnetting is the foundation of network security isolation. A flat network means a single breach compromises everything. Security engineers design subnets to contain breaches and enforce least-privilege access between tiers.

:::objectives
- Calculate subnet masks and broadcast addresses
- Design CIDR-based subnet schemes for security
- Apply segmentation principles to DMZ and internal networks
:::

## Binary and CIDR

IP addresses are 32-bit numbers divided into four 8-bit octets. A subnet mask determines network vs host bits.

CIDR notation: /24 = 255.255.255.0 = 254 usable hosts.

:::info **CIDR Quick Reference**
/24 = 254 hosts, /26 = 62 hosts, /27 = 30 hosts, /28 = 14 hosts, /30 = 2 hosts
:::

## Security Segmentation

Poor design: flat network, attacker reaches everything.
Good design: DMZ, Corporate, Database, Management subnets with firewall rules.

:::summary
- Subnets isolate trust zones and limit blast radius
- Flat networks are dangerous
- Firewall rules enforce least-privilege between segments
:::

:::checkpoint
How many usable hosts in a /28?
A) 16
B) 14
C) 30
D) 8
Correct: B
:::

:::checkpoint
Broadcast of 10.0.1.0/26?
A) 10.0.1.63
B) 10.0.1.255
C) 10.0.1.62
Correct: A
:::
topics.aiPrompt: Design a subnet scheme for a company with web servers, databases, employee workstations, and a management network using CIDR notation
topics.interviewQuestion: What is a subnet mask and how does it relate to CIDR notation?
topics.interviewAnswer: A subnet mask defines which portion of an IP address is the network prefix and which is the host. CIDR notation (e.g., /24) represents the number of leading 1 bits in the mask.

quiz.question: What is the CIDR notation for 255.255.255.0?
How many usable addresses in a /28 network?

What is the broadcast address of 192.168.1.0/24?

topics.title: Day 4: Packet Analysis with Wireshark
topics.description: Use Wireshark to capture, filter, and analyse network traffic for security incidents
topics.content:
## Day 4: Packet Analysis with Wireshark

> **Why This Gets You Hired:** Wireshark is the single most important tool in a network defender’s arsenal. SOC analysts use it to confirm infections, extract IOCs, and reconstruct attacks.

:::objectives
- Capture traffic with BPF filters
- Apply display filters to isolate protocols
- Follow TCP streams to reconstruct conversations
- Identify port scans, ARP spoofing, DNS tunnelling
:::

## Capture vs Display Filters

Capture (BPF): `host 10.0.0.1 and port 80`
Display: `ip.addr == 10.0.0.1 && tcp.port == 80`

## Essential Filters

- `tcp.flags.syn == 1 and tcp.flags.ack == 0` — SYN scan detection
- `http.request.method == "POST"` — data exfiltration
- `arp` — spoofing detection
- `dns and ip.len > 500` — tunnelling

## PCAP Analysis Workflow

1. Protocol Hierarchy overview
2. Endpoints and Conversations
3. Follow TCP Stream for suspicious conversations
4. Extract IOCs

:::summary
- Use capture filters before, display filters after
- Follow TCP Stream is the most powerful feature
- Port scans = multiple SYNs to different ports
- ARP spoofing = duplicate IPs, different MACs
:::

:::checkpoint
What filter shows only SYNs (port scan)?
A) tcp.port == 80
B) tcp.flags.syn == 1 and tcp.flags.ack == 0
C) http.request
Correct: B
:::

:::checkpoint
What reassembles the full TCP conversation?
A) Protocol Hierarchy
B) Follow TCP Stream
C) IO Graph
Correct: B
:::
topics.aiPrompt: Walk through analysing a PCAP file from a malware infection: identify the infected host, C2 server, protocol used, and exfiltrated data
topics.interviewQuestion: How would you identify a port scan in Wireshark?
topics.interviewAnswer: A port scan appears as multiple SYN packets (tcp.flags.syn==1 and tcp.flags.ack==0) to different ports on the same destination IP within a short time window, often followed by RST packets.

What does 'Follow TCP Stream' do?
options: Shows the full application data in one view

topics.title: Day 5: Networking Fundamentals Capstone
topics.description: Apply all networking concepts in a comprehensive security assessment project
topics.content:
## Day 5: Networking Fundamentals Capstone

> **Why This Gets You Hired:** This integrates everything from the week into a real-world scenario. You’ll design a segmented network, write ACLs, and analyse a PCAP.

:::objectives
- Design subnet scheme with default-deny ACLs
- Analyse a PCAP to identify a breach
- Produce a security assessment report
:::

## Scenario

Flat /24 network, no firewall. A workstation is exfiltrating data to an external IP.

## Task 1 — Segmentation Design

DMZ: /27, Corporate: /26, Database: /28, Management: /28

## Task 2 — ACL Rules

Permit HTTP/HTTPS from DMZ to internet, MySQL from web to DB only, deny all else.

## Task 3 — PCAP Analysis

Identify compromised host, exfiltrated data, C2 server. Determine prevention controls.

:::summary
- Flat network = critical vulnerability
- Default-deny ACLs enforce least-privilege
- PCAPs provide definitive evidence
:::
topics.aiPrompt: Given a flat network with an active port scan, design a segmented architecture, write ACL rules, and analyse the PCAP to produce a security report
topics.interviewQuestion: What is the difference between a stateful and stateless firewall?
topics.interviewAnswer: A stateful firewall tracks connection state and allows return traffic automatically, while a stateless firewall filters each packet independently based on ACL rules without connection awareness.
